one_liner: A 20-year-old college student presents with severe headache, high fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and a petechial rash on his trunk and lower extremities developing over 12 hours.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient was in his usual state of health until last evening, when he developed a diffuse headache while studying. Over the next several hours, the headache became severe (10/10), and he developed high fever with chills, nausea, vomiting (3 episodes), and sensitivity to light. His roommate noticed he was confused and difficult to arouse this morning and called EMS. In the ambulance, a petechial rash was noted on his trunk and lower extremities. He lives in a college dormitory and several students in his hall have had upper respiratory infections recently. He received the meningococcal conjugate vaccine (MenACWY) at age 16 but did not receive the serogroup B meningococcal vaccine (MenB). No recent travel, no sick contacts with confirmed this condition.
stage2.social_history: College sophomore living in a dormitory. Social alcohol (binge drinking on weekends). Non-smoker. Sexually active with one partner. No illicit drug use. Up to date on routine vaccinations except MenB.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Acutely ill, febrile, diaphoretic young man, lying on his side with eyes closed, moaning**. HEENT: **Photophobia — winces and turns away from penlight**. Neck: **Severe nuchal rigidity**; **Positive Kernig sign (resistance and pain on knee extension with hip flexed)**; **Positive Brudzinski sign (involuntary hip/knee flexion on passive neck flexion)**. Skin: **Scattered petechiae and purpura on trunk, lower extremities, and axillae**; **some are non-blanching and up to 1 cm**. Neuro: **GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5)**; Confused, oriented to person only; **Follows simple commands intermittently**; No focal neurological deficits; DTRs 2+ symmetric; **Plantars equivocal bilaterally**. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm**; **Capillary refill 3 seconds**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Marked leukocytosis with left shift indicates severe this condition infection
Thrombocytopenia may indicate early DIC, consistent with meningococcemia
Mild hyponatremia possibly from SIADH, which complicates this condition this condition in up to 30% of cases
Mild elevation suggesting prerenal azotemia from sepsis-related hypotension
Markedly elevated; supports severe this condition infection
Markedly elevated; highly suggestive of the suspected diagnosis (vs. viral) infection. Procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL has high sensitivity for this condition this condition.
Elevated serum lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion consistent with sepsis
Mildly prolonged; early coagulopathy raises concern for DIC
Elevated opening pressure consistent with the suspected diagnosis this condition (normal <20 cmH2O)

lab_results.result: 2,400 WBCs/uL (92% neutrophils), protein 280 mg/dL, glucose 18 mg/dL (serum glucose 128)
lab_results.clinical_significance: Neutrophilic pleocytosis, markedly elevated protein, and very low glucose (CSF:serum ratio <0.4) — classic CSF profile for this condition this condition

lab_results.result: Gram-negative diplococci seen on Gram stain. Culture: Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B (pending 24h)
lab_results.clinical_significance: Gram-negative diplococci confirm Neisseria meningitidis. Serogroup B is not covered by the MenACWY vaccine the patient received.

imaging_results.result: No mass effect, midline shift, or hydrocephalus. No hemorrhage. Normal ventricles.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: In this case, normal CT allows safe LP. Note: empiric antibiotics should NEVER be delayed for CT.

key_tests: CSF multiplex PCR panel (rapid pathogen identification)
acceptable_tests: CSF latex agglutination or multiplex PCR
stage3.low_yield_tests_note: Do NOT delay antibiotics for any test — empiric therapy must be given immediately if bacterial meningitis is suspected.

stage5.final_clue: CSF culture grows Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B at 18 hours.

debrief.classic_presentation: Young adult (college dormitory resident) with acute onset of severe headache, high fever, nuchal rigidity, photophobia, and altered mental status developing over hours. Petechial/purpuric rash (meningococcemia) with hemodynamic instability. Kernig and Brudzinski signs positive. CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose, and Gram-negative diplococci.
debrief.pathophysiology: Neisseria meningitidis colonizes the nasopharynx and invades the bloodstream (meningococcemia), disseminating to the meninges. The polysaccharide capsule (serogroups A, B, C, W, Y) evades complement-mediated killing. Bacterial components (lipooligosaccharide/endotoxin) trigger an intense inflammatory cascade in the subarachnoid space: neutrophil recruitment, cytokine release (TNF-alpha, IL-1), increased BBB permeability, cerebral edema, and elevated ICP. The petechial/purpuric rash reflects endothelial damage and DIC from endotoxin-mediated sepsis. Purpura fulminans (widespread purpura with skin necrosis) represents severe DIC with microvascular thrombosis and can lead to Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage and adrenal crisis).
debrief.treatment_overview: IMMEDIATE empiric antibiotics (do NOT delay for any test): Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (or cefotaxime) + vancomycin (until Listeria and resistant pneumococcus excluded). Add ampicillin if age >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant (for Listeria coverage). Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 4 days — give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose (proven mortality benefit for pneumococcal meningitis, recommended empirically for all suspected bacterial meningitis). Aggressive IV fluids and vasopressors for septic shock. Close contact chemoprophylaxis for meningococcal meningitis: ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO x1, or rifampin 600 mg PO q12h x 2 days, or ceftriaxone 250 mg IM x1. Report to public health. Post-recovery audiometry (hearing loss complicates 10-30% of bacterial meningitis).
high_yield_pearls: NEVER delay antibiotics for CT or LP in suspected bacterial meningitis — draw blood cultures and start empiric ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone immediately. Get LP when safe to do so.
Dexamethasone must be given BEFORE or WITH the first antibiotic dose to reduce mortality and hearing loss — giving it after antibiotics provides no benefit.
Petechial/purpuric rash + fever + meningismus = meningococcemia until proven otherwise. This combination should trigger immediate empiric treatment and isolation.
Indications for CT before LP: altered consciousness, focal neurological deficits, papilledema, seizures, immunocompromised state — but if CT is needed, give antibiotics FIRST, then CT, then LP.
Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage) is a devastating complication of meningococcemia — presents with refractory shock, DIC, and purpura fulminans.
sources: IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines for Bacterial Meningitis, 2004 (with 2017 updates) — https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/?q=IDSA+Clinical+Practice+Guidelines+for+Bacterial+Meningitis%2C+2004+%28with+2017+updates%29
Van de Beek D, et al. Bacterial meningitis. Lancet. 2016 — https://www.thelancet.com/action/doSearch?text1=Van+de+Beek+D%2C+et+al.+Bacterial+meningitis.+Lancet.+2016
UpToDate: Bacterial meningitis in adults — clinical features and diagnosis — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search
